county_metadata.record_losses: Courthouse fire 1916 destroyed many early county records
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Haskell County formed 1907 at statehood from the Choctaw Nation, Indian Territory. County seat is Stigler. Named for Charles N. Haskell, first governor of Oklahoma. Courthouse fire 1916 destroyed many early records. Coal mining heritage.
county_metadata.research_tips: Haskell County was part of the Choctaw Nation in Indian Territory. A courthouse fire in 1916 destroyed many early county records. For pre-statehood research, check Choctaw Nation tribal records (Dawes Rolls, allotment records) at NARA Fort Worth and the Choctaw Nation headquarters. For post-fire reconstruction, BLM GLO records, Indian Territory court records at OHS, and church records are essential alternatives. Coal mining in the area brought immigrant workers (Italian, Welsh, Eastern European). The Oklahoma Historical Society holds Indian Territory newspapers and court records. County Clerk records in Stigler are incomplete for the early period due to the fire. FamilySearch has some Oklahoma marriage records. The Stigler Public Library has local genealogical materials.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in Haskell County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

source_profiles.source_name: Haskell County Land Records (post-loss) / Choctaw Nation Allotment Records -- County Clerk / NARA
source_profiles.repository: Haskell County Clerk, Stigler / NARA Fort Worth
source_profiles.coverage_notes: County land records with gaps due to Courthouse fire 1916 destroyed many early county records. Choctaw Nation allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth supplement destroyed county deed books. County Clerk holds surviving and post-loss records from 1907
source_profiles.survival_notes: Incomplete: Courthouse fire 1916 destroyed many early county records. Tribal allotment records at NARA provide critical backup

source_profiles.repository: Haskell County District Court, Stigler
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, inventories, guardianships, and estate settlements; District Court has jurisdiction over probate in Oklahoma; records incomplete due to Courthouse fire 1916 destroyed many early county records
source_profiles.survival_notes: Incomplete: Courthouse fire 1916 destroyed many early county records

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Hughes County formed 1907 at statehood from the Creek (Muscogee) Nation, Indian Territory. County seat is Holdenville. Named for W.C. Hughes. Oil-producing county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Hughes County was part of the Creek (Muscogee) Nation in Indian Territory. Creek Nation enrollment records, Dawes Rolls (Creek by Blood, Creek Freedmen), and allotment records are critical for pre-statehood research. NARA Fort Worth holds Creek Agency records and Bureau of Indian Affairs files. County Clerk records in Holdenville begin at statehood (1907). Oil production in the county generated lease and royalty records. The county had both Creek tribal communities and freedmen communities. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds Indian Territory court records and newspapers. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records. The Hughes County Historical Society has local genealogical materials and cemetery transcriptions.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in Hughes County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Jackson County formed 1907 at statehood from Greer County. County seat is Altus. Named for Andrew Jackson. Inherits dual-state history (formerly Texas). Home to Altus Air Force Base.
county_metadata.research_tips: Jackson County was created at statehood (1907) from Greer County, inheriting the unique dual-state jurisdictional history (formerly Texas territory until 1896 Supreme Court ruling). For pre-1907 records, check Greer County and Texas records (1860-1896). County Clerk records in Altus begin from 1907. BLM GLO land patents are available online. Altus Air Force Base (established as a flight training facility during WWII) has impacted the county's demographics. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds territorial newspapers. The Museum of the Western Prairie in Altus has local history collections. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage records. Cotton farming brought seasonal workers and tenant farmers whose records may be in agricultural census schedules.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Jefferson County formed 1907 at statehood from Comanche County and Chickasaw Nation. County seat is Waurika. Named for Thomas Jefferson. Red River border with Texas.
county_metadata.research_tips: Jefferson County was formed at statehood (1907) from Comanche County and portions of the Chickasaw Nation. Both Chickasaw tribal records (Dawes Rolls, allotment records at NARA Fort Worth) and BLM GLO land patents are relevant depending on the specific area within the county. County Clerk records in Waurika begin from 1907. The county was predominantly agricultural with cattle ranching. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds newspapers and court records. The Jefferson County Historical Society has local genealogical materials and cemetery transcriptions. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records. The Red River border means some families had connections to Texas (Montague and Clay counties).
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in Jefferson County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Johnston County formed 1907 at statehood from the Chickasaw Nation, Indian Territory. County seat is Tishomingo, former capital of the Chickasaw Nation (1856-1907). Named for Douglas H. Johnston, Governor of the Chickasaw Nation.
county_metadata.research_tips: Johnston County was part of the Chickasaw Nation in Indian Territory. Tishomingo, the county seat, served as the capital of the Chickasaw Nation from 1856 to 1907. The Chickasaw Council House Museum in Tishomingo has tribal government records and historical materials. Chickasaw Nation enrollment records, Dawes Rolls (Chickasaw by Blood, Chickasaw Freedmen), and allotment records are at the Chickasaw Nation headquarters in Ada and NARA Fort Worth. County Clerk records begin at statehood (1907). The Oklahoma Historical Society holds Indian Territory court records and newspapers. Johnston County genealogical materials are at the Tishomingo Public Library. Chickasaw Freedmen records are important for African American research. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in Johnston County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

county_metadata.parent_county: Original county, Oklahoma Territory (Cherokee Outlet run)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Kay County formed 1893 from the Cherokee Outlet (land run). County seat is Newkirk. Ponca City is largest city. Multiple tribal communities (Ponca, Kaw, Otoe-Missouria, Tonkawa). Oil industry center.
county_metadata.research_tips: Kay County was formed from the Cherokee Outlet, opened by land run on September 16, 1893. Newkirk is the county seat, but Ponca City is the largest city. County Clerk records begin from 1893. BLM GLO land patents document original Cherokee Outlet claims. The Ponca Tribe, Kaw (Kansa) Nation, Otoe-Missouria Tribe, and Tonkawa Tribe have reservations or communities in the county -- tribal enrollment records at respective tribal offices and NARA are relevant. E.W. Marland's oil empire was based in Ponca City -- oil company records may document employees. The Marland Mansion and Pioneer Woman Museum have local history materials. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds territorial newspapers. The Kay County Genealogical Society has published cemetery transcriptions. FamilySearch has Oklahoma territorial and state marriage records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Kay County; County Clerk deed books from 1893

county_metadata.parent_county: Original county, Oklahoma Territory (1889 Land Run)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Kingfisher County formed 1890 as original Oklahoma Territory county from the 1889 Land Run. County seat is Kingfisher. Named for King Fisher Stage Coach stop. Chisholm Trail heritage.
county_metadata.research_tips: Kingfisher County was one of the original Oklahoma Territory counties formed after the 1889 Land Run. County Clerk records begin from 1889, among the oldest in Oklahoma. BLM GLO land patents document original Land Run claims. The Chisholm Trail passed through the county, and pre-Land Run ranching records may be available. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds territorial newspapers, census records, and court records. The Kingfisher Free Library and Kingfisher County Historical Society have local genealogical collections. German-born settlers established churches -- check Catholic and Lutheran parish records. FamilySearch has Oklahoma territorial and state marriage records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Kingfisher County; County Clerk deed books from 1890

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Kiowa County formed 1901 after the KCA lands lottery. County seat is Hobart. Named for the Kiowa people. Located in southwestern Oklahoma.
county_metadata.research_tips: Kiowa County was organized in 1901 after the Kiowa-Comanche-Apache (KCA) lands were opened by lottery. NARA Fort Worth holds extensive records for the Kiowa, Comanche, and Apache tribes. The county is named for the Kiowa people. County Clerk records in Hobart begin from 1901. BLM GLO land patents document original settler claims. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds territorial court records and newspapers. The Kiowa County Historical Society in Hobart has local materials. FamilySearch has Oklahoma county marriage and probate records. The Mountain View area has significant Kiowa tribal heritage -- check tribal enrollment offices and the Kiowa Tribal Museum.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Kiowa County; County Clerk deed books from 1901

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Latimer County formed 1907 at statehood from the Choctaw Nation, Indian Territory. County seat is Wilburton. Named for James S. Latimer. Coal mining heritage with immigrant communities.
county_metadata.research_tips: Latimer County was part of the Choctaw Nation in Indian Territory. Coal mining was a significant industry, attracting Italian, Welsh, and Eastern European immigrants. Choctaw Nation tribal records (Dawes Rolls, allotment records) at NARA Fort Worth and the Choctaw Nation headquarters are essential for pre-statehood research. Mining company records (employment, payroll, accident reports) may survive at the Oklahoma Historical Society. County Clerk records in Wilburton begin at statehood (1907). Eastern Oklahoma State College in Wilburton has some regional history materials. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds Indian Territory court records and newspapers. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records. Cemetery records reflect the diverse ethnic heritage of mining communities.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in Latimer County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

source_profiles.source_name: Dawes Rolls -- Choctaw Nation Enrollment Records / NARA
source_profiles.repository: NARA Fort Worth / Choctaw Nation / Oklahoma Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Dawes Commission enrollment records for Choctaw citizens (Choctaw by Blood, Choctaw Freedmen) in Latimer County area; includes application jackets with family information, census cards, and land allotment details; essential for tracing Choctaw families before and after statehood

source_profiles.source_name: Latimer County Federal and Tribal Census Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry / Oklahoma Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1900-1950 for Latimer County; Choctaw Nation census records and Indian Territory schedules at OHS and NARA

source_profiles.source_name: Latimer County Coal Mining Records -- District Court / OHS
source_profiles.repository: Latimer County District Court, Wilburton / Oklahoma Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Coal mining records for Latimer County including mine inspector reports, accident records, worker compensation files, and union records; diverse immigrant mining communities (Italian, Welsh, Eastern European); naturalization records for immigrant miners at District Court; OHS holds mine inspector reports

source_profiles.source_name: Latimer County Marriage Records -- County Clerk / OHS / FamilySearch
source_profiles.repository: Latimer County Clerk, Wilburton / Oklahoma Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses and records for Latimer County from 1907; County Clerk issued marriage licenses in Oklahoma; OHS holds some county marriage records on microfilm; FamilySearch has indexed Oklahoma marriages; state vital records registration began 1908

source_profiles.source_name: Latimer County Indian Territory Court Records -- OHS / NARA
source_profiles.repository: Oklahoma Historical Society / NARA Fort Worth
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Indian Territory court records for the Latimer County area; US courts for Indian Territory (Northern, Central, Southern districts) handled criminal and civil cases; OHS holds court records, case files, and dockets; NARA Fort Worth has federal court records

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Le Flore County formed 1907 at statehood from the Choctaw Nation, Indian Territory. County seat is Poteau. Named for the Le Flore family, prominent Choctaw leaders. Spiro Mounds archaeological site. Coal mining and timber industries.
county_metadata.research_tips: Le Flore County was part of the Choctaw Nation in Indian Territory. The county has extensive Choctaw heritage with pre-statehood records including Dawes Rolls (Choctaw by Blood, Choctaw Freedmen), allotment records, and tribal court records at NARA Fort Worth and the Choctaw Nation headquarters. The Spiro Mounds Archaeological Center documents pre-contact Native American civilization. Coal mining and timber industries attracted diverse immigrant populations. County Clerk records in Poteau begin at statehood (1907). The Oklahoma Historical Society holds Indian Territory newspapers and court records. Carl Albert State College in Poteau has regional history collections. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records. The Le Flore County Genealogical Society has published cemetery transcriptions.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in Le Flore County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

source_profiles.source_name: Le Flore County Deed Books / Choctaw Nation Allotment Records -- County Clerk / NARA
source_profiles.repository: Le Flore County Clerk, Poteau / NARA Fort Worth
source_profiles.coverage_notes: County deed books for Le Flore County from 1907 (statehood); pre-statehood land was held as Choctaw Nation allotments under the Dawes Act -- allotment records at NARA Fort Worth and OHS; County Clerk maintains deeds, mortgages, and liens
source_profiles.survival_notes: County records complete from 1907; allotment records at NARA are substantially complete
